name: Jane Jacobs
practice: Urban observation, community-scale economics, anti-demolition activism
approach: Eyes on the street. The city is not a problem to be solved by experts — it's a living system understood by watching how people actually use it. Diversity of use, short blocks, old buildings, density of people. These are not disorder — they are the immune system of a neighborhood.
body_of_work: The Death and Life of Great American Cities (1961), The Economy of Cities (1969), Systems of Survival (1992). Stopped the Lower Manhattan Expressway. Fought Robert Moses. Proved that small-scale, mixed-use, high-density neighborhoods outperform planned superblocks.
why_instructive: She saw what the sphere schema sees — a place is a living system with layers. Her fight against Moses is the fight against treating places as problems rather than organisms.

dome_relevance.5: Housing as community fabric, not units
dome_relevance.8: Community as emergent property of good design

sphere_relevance.1: Zoning critique — mixed use over segregated use
sphere_relevance.4: Economic vitality from diversity, not monoculture
sphere_relevance.5: Social fabric emerges from physical design
sphere_relevance.6: Temporal depth — old buildings subsidize new uses
sphere_relevance.7: Activation as natural byproduct of good urban form
sphere_relevance.10: Catalyst — one good block activates the next
